# 8\. Containerized deployment [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#containerized-deployment "Link to this heading")

There are many ways to use containerized application, from the most
simple (simple Docker images) to sophisticated (Kubernetes and so on).

Note

This kind of deployment needs the
[docker application](https://www.docker.com/) to be installed and
running.
Check this [tutorial](https://www.docker.com/101-tutorial).

Hint

Docker run pre packaged application (aka images)
which can be retrieved as sources (Dockerfile and resources) to
build or already built from registries (private or public).

Note

QGIS Debian-Ubuntu package downloads need a valid gpg authentication key.
Please refer to the [installation pages](https://www.qgis.org/resources/installation-guide/#debian--ubuntu)
to update the following Dockerfile.

## [8.1. Simple docker images](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id5) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#simple-docker-images "Link to this heading")

As the docker image does not exist in a public registry. you will need to build
it. To do so create a directory `qgis-server` and within its directory:

- create a file `Dockerfile` with this content:


```
FROM debian:bookworm-slim

ENV LANG=en_EN.UTF-8

RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install --no-install-recommends --no-install-suggests --allow-unauthenticated -y \
        gnupg \
        ca-certificates \
        wget \
        locales \
    && localedef -i en_US -f UTF-8 en_US.UTF-8 \
    # Add the current key for package downloading
    # Please refer to QGIS install documentation (https://www.qgis.org/fr/site/forusers/alldownloads.html#debian-ubuntu)
    && mkdir -m755 -p /etc/apt/keyrings \
    && wget -O /etc/apt/keyrings/qgis-archive-keyring.gpg https://qgis.org/download//downloads/qgis-archive-keyring.gpg \
    # Add repository for latest version of qgis-server
    # Please refer to QGIS repositories documentation if you want other version (https://qgis.org/resources/installation-guide/#repositories)
    && echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/qgis-archive-keyring.gpg] https://qgis.org/debian bookworm main" | tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/qgis.list \
    && apt-get update \
    && apt-get install --no-install-recommends --no-install-suggests --allow-unauthenticated -y \
        qgis-server \
        spawn-fcgi \
        xauth \
        xvfb \
    && apt-get remove --purge -y \
        gnupg \
        wget \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN useradd -m qgis

ENV TINI_VERSION v0.19.0
ADD https://github.com/krallin/tini/releases/download/${TINI_VERSION}/tini /tini
RUN chmod +x /tini

ENV QGIS_PREFIX_PATH /usr
ENV QGIS_SERVER_LOG_STDERR 1
ENV QGIS_SERVER_LOG_LEVEL 2

COPY cmd.sh /home/qgis/cmd.sh
RUN chmod -R 777 /home/qgis/cmd.sh
RUN chown qgis:qgis /home/qgis/cmd.sh

USER qgis
WORKDIR /home/qgis

ENTRYPOINT ["/tini", "--"]

CMD ["/home/qgis/cmd.sh"]
```

Copy to clipboard

- create a file `cmd.sh` with this content:


```
#!/bin/bash

[[ $DEBUG == "1" ]] && env

exec /usr/bin/xvfb-run --auto-servernum --server-num=1 /usr/bin/spawn-fcgi -p 5555 -n -d /home/qgis -- /usr/lib/cgi-bin/qgis_mapserv.fcgi
```

Copy to clipboard

- build the image with:


```
docker build -f Dockerfile -t qgis-server ./
```

Copy to clipboard

### [8.1.1. First run](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id6) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#first-run "Link to this heading")

To run the server you will need a QGIS project file. You can use one of yours or pick
[this sample](https://gitlab.com/Oslandia/qgis/docker-qgis/-/blob/cc1798074d4a66a472721352f3984bb318777a5a/qgis-exec/data/osm.qgs).

To do so, create a directory `data` within the directory
`qgis-server` and copy your file in it. To comply with the following
explanations, rename it to `osm.qgs`.

Note

You may need to add advertised URLs under the QGIS Server tab of the
Project ► Properties if the GetCapabilites are broken.
For example if your server is exposed on port 8080, you will put this for
advertised URL `http://localhost:8080/qgis-server/`. More information
available in section [Configure your project](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/getting_started.html#creatingwmsfromproject) and subsequent.

Now, you can run the server with:

```
docker network create qgis
docker run -d --rm --name qgis-server --net=qgis --hostname=qgis-server \
              -v $(pwd)/data:/data:ro -p 5555:5555 \
              -e "QGIS_PROJECT_FILE=/data/osm.qgs" \
              qgis-server
```

Copy to clipboard

Options used:

- **-d**: run in the background

- **–rm**: remove the container when it is stopped

- **–name**: name of the container to be created

- **–net**: (previously created) sub network

- **–hostname**: container hostname, for later referencing

- **-v**: local data directory to be mounted in the container

- **-p**: host/container port mapping

- **-e**: environment variable to be used in the container


To check, type `docker ps | grep qgis-server` and you should see a
line with **qgis-server**:

```
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE         COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                    NAMES
4de8192da76e   qgis-server   "/tini -- /home/qgis…"   3 seconds ago   Up 2 seconds   0.0.0.0:5555->5555/tcp   qgis-server
```

Copy to clipboard

### [8.1.2. Usable sample](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id7) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#usable-sample "Link to this heading")

As the server is only accepting fastcgi connections, you need
an HTTP server that handles this protocol.
To do so we have to create a simple Nginx configuration file and start
a Nginx image.

Create a file `nginx.conf` in the current directory with this
content:

```
server {
  listen 80;
  server_name _;
  location / {
    root  /usr/share/nginx/html;
    index index.html index.htm;
  }
  location /qgis-server {
    proxy_buffers 16 16k;
    proxy_buffer_size 16k;
    gzip off;
    include fastcgi_params;
    fastcgi_pass qgis-server:5555;
  }
}
```

Copy to clipboard

And type this command:

```
docker run -d --rm --name nginx --net=qgis --hostname=nginx \
              -v $(pwd)/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf:ro -p 8080:80 \
              nginx:1.13
```

Copy to clipboard

To check capabilities availability, type in a browser
[http://localhost:8080/qgis-server/?SERVICE=WMS&VERSION=1.3.0&REQUEST=GetCapabilities](http://localhost:8080/qgis-server/?SERVICE=WMS&VERSION=1.3.0&REQUEST=GetCapabilities)

### [8.1.3. Cleanup](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id8) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#cleanup "Link to this heading")

To cleanup the running images, type:

```
docker stop qgis-server nginx
```

Copy to clipboard

## [8.2. Docker stacks](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id9) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#docker-stacks "Link to this heading")

The previous method is scriptable, but not easily packageable nor
standardized or easily manageable.

To work with a docker image set you could use a docker stack managed
by an orchestrator.
In a stack, the images are working in the same private network, and
you can start / stop the whole stack or deploy the stack to other
workers.
There are many orchestrators, for example Swarm, Kubernetes and Mesos.

In the following, we will present simple configurations for testing
purposes.
They are not suitable for production.

### [8.2.1. Swarm/docker-compose](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id10) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#swarm-docker-compose "Link to this heading")

Docker now has its own orchestrator: Swarm (compatible with docker-compose
files). You have to
[enable it](https://docs.docker.com/guides/orchestration/#enable-docker-swarm)
(the Mac version will also work with Linux).

#### [Stack description](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id11) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#stack-description "Link to this heading")

Now that you have Swarm working, create the service file (see
[Deploy to Swarm](https://docs.docker.com/guides/swarm-deploy/))
`qgis-stack.yaml`:

```
version: '3.7'

services:
  qgis-server:
    # Should use version with utf-8 locale support:
    image: qgis-server:latest
    volumes:
      - REPLACE_WITH_FULL_PATH/data:/data:ro
    environment:
      - LANG=en_EN.UTF-8
      - QGIS_PROJECT_FILE=/data/osm.qgs
      - QGIS_SERVER_LOG_LEVEL=0  # INFO (log all requests)
      - DEBUG=1                  # display env before spawning QGIS Server

  nginx:
    image: nginx:1.13
    ports:
      - 8080:80
    volumes:
      - REPLACE_WITH_FULL_PATH/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf:ro
    depends_on:
      - qgis-server
```

Copy to clipboard

To deploy (or update) the stack, type:

```
docker stack deploy -c qgis-stack.yaml qgis-stack
```

Copy to clipboard

Check the stack deployment status until you obtain **1/1** in the
**replicas** column:

```
docker stack services qgis-stack
```

Copy to clipboard

Something like:

```
ID                  NAME                MODE                REPLICAS            IMAGE               PORTS
gmx7ewlvwsqt        qgis_nginx          replicated          1/1                 nginx:1.13          *:8080->80/tcp
l0v2e7cl43u3        qgis_qgis-server    replicated          1/1                 qgis-server:latest
```

Copy to clipboard

To check WMS capabilities, type in a web browser
[http://localhost:8080/qgis-server/?SERVICE=WMS&VERSION=1.3.0&REQUEST=GetCapabilities](http://localhost:8080/qgis-server/?SERVICE=WMS&VERSION=1.3.0&REQUEST=GetCapabilities)

#### [Cleanup](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id12) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id3 "Link to this heading")

To cleanup, type:

```
docker stack rm qgis-stack
```

Copy to clipboard

### [8.2.2. Kubernetes](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id13) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#kubernetes "Link to this heading")

#### [Installation](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id14) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#installation "Link to this heading")

If you have a **Docker Desktop** installation, using Kubernetes (aka
k8s) is pretty straight forward:
[enable k8s](https://docs.docker.com/guides/orchestration/#turn-on-kubernetes).

If not, follow the
[minikube tutorial](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/hello-minikube/)
or
[microk8s for Ubuntu](https://canonical.com/microk8s/docs/getting-started).

As Kubernetes installation can be really complex, we will only focus
on aspects used by this demo.
For further / deeper information, check the
[official documentation](https://kubernetes.io/docs/home/).

##### microk8s [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#microk8s "Link to this heading")

microk8s needs extra steps: you have to enable the registry and tag
the qgis-server image in order to have Kubernetes to find the created
images.

First, enable the registry:

```
microk8s enable dashboard dns registry
```

Copy to clipboard

Then, tag and push the image to your newly created registry:

```
docker tag qgis-server 127.0.0.1:32000/qgis-server && docker push 127.0.0.1:32000/qgis-server
```

Copy to clipboard

Finally, add or complete the `/etc/docker/daemon.json` to have
your registry **127.0.0.1:32000** listed in the
**insecure-registries** field:

```
{
  "insecure-registries": ["127.0.0.1:32000"]
}
```

Copy to clipboard

#### [Creating manifests](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id15) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#creating-manifests "Link to this heading")

Kubernetes describes the objects to deploy in yaml manifests.
There are many different kinds, but we will only use deployments
(handle pods, i.e. docker images) and services to expose the
deployments to internal or external purposes.

##### Deployment manifests [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#deployment-manifests "Link to this heading")

Create a file `deployments.yaml` with this content:

```
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: qgis-server
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      myLabel: qgis-server
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        myLabel: qgis-server
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: qgis-server
          image: localhost:32000/qgis-server:latest
          imagePullPolicy: Always
          env:
            - name: LANG
              value: en_EN.UTF-8
            - name: QGIS_PROJECT_FILE
              value: /data/osm.qgs
            - name: QGIS_SERVER_LOG_LEVEL
              value: "0"
            - name: DEBUG
              value: "1"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5555
          volumeMounts:
            - name: qgis-data
              mountPath: /data/
      volumes:
        - name: qgis-data
          hostPath:
            path: REPLACE_WITH_FULL_PATH/data

---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: qgis-nginx
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      myLabel: qgis-nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        myLabel: qgis-nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: qgis-nginx
          image: nginx:1.13
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
          volumeMounts:
            - name: nginx-conf
              mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d/
      volumes:
        - name: nginx-conf
          configMap:
            name: nginx-configuration

---
kind: ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
metadata:
  name: nginx-configuration
data:
  nginx.conf: |
    server {
      listen 80;
      server_name _;
      location / {
        root  /usr/share/nginx/html;
        index index.html index.htm;
      }
      location /qgis-server {
        proxy_buffers 16 16k;
        proxy_buffer_size 16k;
        gzip off;
        include fastcgi_params;
        fastcgi_pass qgis-server:5555;
        }
      }
```

Copy to clipboard

##### Service manifests [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#service-manifests "Link to this heading")

Create a file `services.yaml` with this content:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: qgis-server
  namespace: default
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    myLabel: qgis-server
  ports:
    - port: 5555
      targetPort: 5555
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: qgis-nginx
  namespace: default
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    myLabel: qgis-nginx
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 80
      nodePort: 30080
```

Copy to clipboard

#### [Deploying manifests](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id16) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#deploying-manifests "Link to this heading")

To deploy the images and services in Kubernetes, one can use the
dashboard (click on the **+** on the upper right) or the command line.

Note

When using the command line with microk8s you will have to prefix
each command with microk8s.

To deploy or update your manifests:

```
kubectl apply -f ./
```

Copy to clipboard

To check what is currently deployed:

```
kubectl get pods,services,deployment
```

Copy to clipboard

You should obtain something like:

```
NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/qgis-nginx-54845ff6f6-8skp9    1/1     Running   0          27m
pod/qgis-server-75df8ddd89-c7t7s   1/1     Running   0          27m

NAME                       TYPE        CLUSTER-IP       EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
service/Kubernetes         ClusterIP   10.152.183.1     <none>        443/TCP        5h51m
service/qgis-exec-server   ClusterIP   10.152.183.218   <none>        5555/TCP       35m
service/qgis-nginx         NodePort    10.152.183.234   <none>        80:30080/TCP   27m
service/qgis-server        ClusterIP   10.152.183.132   <none>        5555/TCP       27m

NAME                          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/qgis-nginx    1/1     1            1           27m
deployment.apps/qgis-server   1/1     1            1           27m
```

Copy to clipboard

To read nginx/qgis logs, type:

```
kubectl logs -f POD_NAME
```

Copy to clipboard

To check WMS capabilities, type in a web browser
[http://localhost:30080/qgis-server/?SERVICE=WMS&VERSION=1.3.0&REQUEST=GetCapabilities](http://localhost:30080/qgis-server/?SERVICE=WMS&VERSION=1.3.0&REQUEST=GetCapabilities)

#### [Cleanup](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id17) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id4 "Link to this heading")

To clean up, type:

```
kubectl delete service/qgis-server service/qgis-nginx deployment/qgis-nginx deployment/qgis-server configmap/nginx-configuration
```

Copy to clipboard

## [8.3. Cloud deployment](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id18) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#cloud-deployment "Link to this heading")

Managing your own cluster of servers to handle the deployment of
containerized applications, is a complex job.
You have to handle multiple issues, such as hardware, bandwidth and
security at different levels.

Cloud deployment solutions can be a good alternative when you do not
want to focus on infrastructure management.

A cloud deployment may use proprietary mechanisms, but they are also
compatible with the stages explained previously
( [docker images](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html#simple-docker-images) and
[stack management](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html#docker-stacks)).

### [8.3.1. AWS usecase](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#id19) [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html\#aws-usecase "Link to this heading")

With Amazon AWS, through
[ECS (Elastic Container Service)](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
functionalities, you can use docker-compose or Kubernetes compatible
wrappers to manage your stack.
You will have to create an
[image registry](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecr/home) for your
custom images to be accessible.

To use docker-compose alike functionalities, you need to install the
**ecs-cli** client and have [proper permissions / roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html).
Then, with the help of the [ecs-cli compose](https://github.com/aws/amazon-ecs-cli) commands,
you can reuse the [stack description](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html#docker-compose-file).

To use Kubernetes, you can use the AWS web console or the command line
tool
[eksctl](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/getting-started.html)
and have the proper permissions / roles. Then with a well configured
kubectl environment, you can reuse the [Kubernetes manifests](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/server_manual/containerized_deployment.html#k8s-manifests).

QGIS Documentation

web.title: 18.2. QGIS as OGC Data Server
web.description: QGIS Server is an open source WMS 1.3, WFS 1.0.0, WFS 1.1.0 and WCS 1.1.1 implementation that, in addition, implements advanced cartographic ...
web.markdown:
- [Home](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/index.html)
- [QGIS User Guide](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/index.html)
- [18\. Working with OGC Data](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/index.html)
- 18.2. QGIS as OGC Data Server
- [View page source](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/_sources/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/index.rst.txt)

[Previous](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/ogc_client_support.html "18.1. QGIS as OGC Data Client") [Next](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/getting_started.html "18.2.1. Getting Started")

* * *

# 18.2. QGIS as OGC Data Server [](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/index.html\#qgis-as-ogc-data-server "Link to this heading")

QGIS Server is an open source WMS 1.3, WFS 1.0.0, WFS 1.1.0 and WCS 1.1.1
implementation that, in addition, implements advanced cartographic features for
thematic mapping. QGIS Server is a FastCGI/CGI (Common Gateway Interface)
application written in C++ that works together with a web server (e.g., Apache,
Nginx). It has Python plugin support allowing for fast and efficient
development and deployment of new features.

QGIS Server uses QGIS as back end for the GIS logic and for map rendering.
Furthermore, the Qt library is used for graphics and for platform-independent
C++ programming. In contrast to other WMS software, the QGIS Server uses
cartographic rules as a configuration language, both for the server
configuration and for the user-defined cartographic rules.

As QGIS desktop and QGIS Server use the same visualization libraries, the maps
that are published on the web look the same as in desktop GIS.

In the following sections, we will provide a sample configuration to set up
a QGIS Server on Linux (Debian, Ubuntu and derivatives) and on Windows.
For more information about server plugin development, please read [QGIS Server and Python](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/pyqgis_developer_cookbook/server.html#server-plugins).

- [18.2.1. Getting Started](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/getting_started.html)
  - [18.2.1.1. Installation on Debian-based systems](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/getting_started.html#installation-on-debian-based-systems)
  - [18.2.1.2. Installation on Windows](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/getting_started.html#installation-on-windows)
  - [18.2.1.3. Serve a project](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/getting_started.html#serve-a-project)
  - [18.2.1.4. Configure your project](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/getting_started.html#configure-your-project)
- [18.2.2. Services](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html)
  - [18.2.2.1. Web Map Service (WMS)](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#web-map-service-wms)
  - [18.2.2.2. Web Feature Service (WFS)](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#web-feature-service-wfs)
  - [18.2.2.3. Web Map Tile Service (WMTS)](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#web-map-tile-service-wmts)
  - [18.2.2.4. WFS3 (OGC API Features)](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#wfs3-ogc-api-features)
  - [18.2.2.5. Extra parameters supported by all request types](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#extra-parameters-supported-by-all-request-types)
  - [18.2.2.6. REDLINING](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#redlining)
  - [18.2.2.7. External WMS layers](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/services.html#external-wms-layers)
- [18.2.3. Plugins](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/plugins.html)
  - [18.2.3.1. Installation](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/plugins.html#installation)
  - [18.2.3.2. HTTP Server configuration](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/plugins.html#http-server-configuration)
  - [18.2.3.3. How to use a plugin](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/plugins.html#how-to-use-a-plugin)
- [18.2.4. Advanced configuration](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html)
  - [18.2.4.1. Logging](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html#logging)
  - [18.2.4.2. Environment variables](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html#environment-variables)
  - [18.2.4.3. Settings summary](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html#settings-summary)
  - [18.2.4.4. Short name for layers, groups and project](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html#short-name-for-layers-groups-and-project)
  - [18.2.4.5. Connection to service file](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html#connection-to-service-file)
  - [18.2.4.6. Add fonts to your linux server](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/config.html#add-fonts-to-your-linux-server)
- [18.2.5. Containerized deployment](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/containerized_deployment.html)
  - [18.2.5.1. Simple docker images](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/containerized_deployment.html#simple-docker-images)
  - [18.2.5.2. Docker stacks](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/containerized_deployment.html#docker-stacks)
  - [18.2.5.3. Cloud deployment](https://docs.qgis.org/3.10/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_ogc/server/containerized_deployment.html#cloud-deployment)

web.title: Container Registry & Runtime (Docker Deploys)
web.description: Heroku Container Registry allows you to deploy your Docker images to Heroku. Both Common Runtime and Private Spaces are supported.
web.markdown:
# Container Registry & Runtime (Docker Deploys)

## Table of Contents \[expand\]

- [Getting started](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#getting-started)
- [Logging in to the registry](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#logging-in-to-the-registry)
- [Building and pushing image(s)](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#building-and-pushing-image-s)
- [Releasing an image](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#releasing-an-image)
- [One-off dynos](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#one-off-dynos)
- [Using a CI/CD platform](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#using-a-ci-cd-platform)
- [Release phase](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#release-phase)
- [Dockerfile commands and runtime](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#dockerfile-commands-and-runtime)
- [Testing an image locally](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#testing-an-image-locally)
- [Heroku base images](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#heroku-base-images)
- [Changing deployment method](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#changing-deployment-method)
- [Known issues and limitations](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#known-issues-and-limitations)

Last updated November 05, 2025

Heroku Container Registry allows you to deploy your Docker images to Heroku. Both [Common Runtime](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/dyno-runtime#common-runtime) and [Private Spaces](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/private-spaces) are supported.

If you would like Heroku to build your Docker images, as well as take advantage of Review Apps, check out [building Docker images with heroku.yml](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/build-docker-images-heroku-yml).

The Heroku `container` [stack](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/stack) is intended for advanced use-cases only. Unless you have a specific need for custom
Docker images, we recommend using Heroku’s default [buildpack](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/buildpacks)-powered build system instead, which
offers automatic base image security updates, language-specific optimisations, and avoids the need to maintain a `Dockerfile`.

This article is only applicable to [Cedar](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/generations#cedar)-generation apps.

## [Getting started](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#getting-started)

Make sure you have a working Docker installation (eg. `docker ps`) and that you’re logged in to Heroku (`heroku login`).

Log in to Container Registry:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:login
```

Get sample code by cloning an Alpine-based python example:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ git clone https://github.com/heroku/alpinehelloworld.git
```

Navigate to the app’s directory and create a Heroku app:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ cd alpinehelloworld
$ heroku create --stack container
Creating salty-fortress-4191... done, stack is container
https://salty-fortress-4191.herokuapp.com/ | https://git.heroku.com/salty-fortress-4191.git
```

Build the image and push to Container Registry:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:push web
```

Then release the image to your app:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:release web
```

Now open the app in your browser:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku open
```

## [Logging in to the registry](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#logging-in-to-the-registry)

Heroku runs a container registry on `registry.heroku.com`.

If you are using the Heroku CLI, you can log in with:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:login
```

or directly via the Docker CLI:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ docker login --username=_ --password=$(heroku auth:token) registry.heroku.com
```

## [Building and pushing image(s)](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#building-and-pushing-image-s)

Make sure your app’s stack is set to `container` via `heroku stack:set container`

### [Build an image and push](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#build-an-image-and-push)

To build an image and push it to Container Registry, make sure that your directory contains a Dockerfile and run:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:push <process-type>
```

### [Pushing an existing image](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#pushing-an-existing-image)

To push an image to Heroku, such as one pulled from Docker Hub, tag it and push it according to this naming template:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ docker tag <image> registry.heroku.com/<app>/<process-type>
$ docker push registry.heroku.com/<app>/<process-type>
```

By specifying the process type in the tag, you can [release the image using the CLI](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#cli). If you would prefer to not specify the process type in the tag, you’ll have to [release via the API](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#api) which uses the `image_id`.

### [Pushing multiple images](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#pushing-multiple-images)

To push multiple images, rename your Dockerfiles using `Dockerfile.<process-type>`:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ ls -R

./webapp:
Dockerfile.web

./worker:
Dockerfile.worker

./image-processor:
Dockerfile.image
```

Then, from the root directory of the project, run:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:push --recursive
=== Building web
=== Building worker
=== Building image
=== Pushing web
=== Pushing worker
=== Pushing image
```

This will build and push all 3 images. If you only want to push specific images, you can specify the process types:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:push web worker --recursive
=== Building web
=== Building worker
=== Pushing web
=== Pushing worker
```

## [Releasing an image](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#releasing-an-image)

### [CLI](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#cli)

After you’ve successfully pushed an image to Container Registry, you can create a new release using:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:release web
```

If you have multiple images, list them:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:release web worker
```

In an app with multiple process types, if you only release one process type (e.g., `heroku container:release web`), all process types will be restarted.

### [API](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#api)

Copy code to clipboard

```bash
curl --netrc -X PATCH https://api.heroku.com/apps/$APP_ID_OR_NAME/formation \
  -d '{
  "updates": [\
    {\
      "type": "web",\
      "docker_image": "$WEB_DOCKER_IMAGE_ID"\
    },\
    {\
      "type": "worker",\
      "docker_image": "$WORKER_DOCKER_IMAGE_ID"\
    }\
  ]
}' \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.heroku+json; version=3.docker-releases"
```

For the `curl --netrc` option to work, you must have previously run `heroku login` to populate the API token in your `.netrc` file.

### [Getting a Docker image ID](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#getting-a-docker-image-id)

The docker image values used when releasing an image via the platform API need to be in the format `algorithm:hex`. For example:

```
sha256:4d2647aab0e8fbe92cb0fc88c500eb51661c5907f4f14e79efe8bfbda1f7d159
```

To get this ID for your image you can run the following command:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ docker inspect my_image --format={{.Id}}
sha256:4d2647aab0e8fbe92cb0fc88c500eb51661c5907f4f14e79efe8bfbda1f7d159
```

## [One-off dynos](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#one-off-dynos)

If your app is composed of multiple Docker images, you can target the process type when creating a one-off dyno:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku run bash --type=worker
Running bash on ⬢ multidockerfile... up, worker.5180
$
```

If the type is not specified, the`web` image is used.

## [Using a CI/CD platform](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#using-a-ci-cd-platform)

Currently, it is not possible to use Heroku CI to test container builds.

If you are using a third party CI/CD platform, you can push images to the registry. First authenticate with the following information:

- Registry URL: `registry.heroku.com`
- Username: `your Heroku email address`
- Email: `your Heroku email address`
- Password: `your Heroku API key`

Many CI/CD providers have documentation about how to build and push images to a Docker registry:

- [Bamboo](https://confluence.atlassian.com/bamboo/configuring-the-docker-task-in-bamboo-720411254.html#ConfiguringtheDockertaskinBamboo-push)
- [Codefresh](https://codefresh.io/docs/docs/yaml-examples/examples/deploy-to-heroku/)
- [Codeship](https://docs.cloudbees.com/docs/cloudbees-codeship/latest/)
- [CircleCI](https://circleci.com/docs/custom-images/#storing-images-in-a-docker-registry)
- [Jenkins](https://plugins.jenkins.io/docker-build-publish/)
- [Semaphore CI](https://semaphoreci.com/community/tutorials/continuous-deployment-of-a-python-flask-application-with-docker-and-semaphore)
- [TravisCI](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/docker/#Pushing-a-Docker-Image-to-a-Registry)

## [Release phase](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#release-phase)

To use [release phase](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/release-phase) push a Docker image named `release`:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:push release
```

When you release your Docker images, by running `heroku container:release`, your release phase process type needs to be specified:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku container:release web release
Releasing images web,release to your-app-name... done
Running release command...
Migrating database.
```

If you would like to see streaming logs as release phase executes, your Docker image is required to have `curl`. If your Docker image does not include `curl`, release phase logs will only be available in your application logs.

## [Dockerfile commands and runtime](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#dockerfile-commands-and-runtime)

Docker images run in [dynos the same way that slugs do](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/dynos), and under the same constraints:

- The web process must listen for HTTP traffic on `$PORT`, which is set by Heroku. `EXPOSE` in `Dockerfile` is not respected, but can be used for local testing. Only HTTP requests are supported.
- Network linking of dynos is not supported.
- The filesystem is ephemeral.
- The working directory is `/`. You can set a different directory using `WORKDIR`.
- `ENV`, for setting environment variables, is supported.


  - We suggest using `ENV` for runtime variables (e.g., `GEM_PATH`) and `heroku config` for credentials, so that sensitive credentials aren’t accidentally checked into source code control.
- `ENTRYPOINT` is optional. If not set, `/bin/sh -c` will be used


  - `CMD` will always be executed by a shell so that [config vars](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/config-vars) are made available to your process; to execute single binaries or use images without a shell please use `ENTRYPOINT`

We strongly recommend testing images locally as a non-root user, as **containers are not run with root privileges on Heroku**.

### [Unsupported Dockerfile commands](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#unsupported-dockerfile-commands)

- `VOLUME` \- Volume mounting is not supported. The [filesystem of the dyno is ephemeral](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/dynos#ephemeral-filesystem).
- `EXPOSE` \- While `EXPOSE` can be used for local testing, it is not supported in Heroku’s container runtime. Instead your web process/code should get the $PORT environment variable.
- `STOPSIGNAL` \- The dyno manager will request that your processes shut down gracefully by sending them a [SIGTERM signal](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/dyno-shutdown-behavior#graceful-shutdown-example) followed by a SIGKILL signal. `STOPSIGNAL` is not respected.
- `SHELL` \- The default shell for Docker images is `/bin/sh`, you can override with `ENTRYPOINT` if necessary.
- `HEALTHCHECK` \- While `HEALTHCHECK` is not currently supported, the Heroku Dyno manager automatically checks the health of your running containers.

## [Testing an image locally](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#testing-an-image-locally)

When testing an image locally there are a number of best practices. These best practices are implemented in this [example Dockerfile](https://github.com/heroku/alpinehelloworld/blob/master/Dockerfile).

### [Run the image as a non-root user](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#run-the-image-as-a-non-root-user)

We strongly recommend testing images locally as a non-root user, as containers are not run with root privileges in Heroku. Immediately before `CMD` you can add the following commands to your Dockerfile:

If using Alpine:

```
RUN adduser -D myuser
USER myuser
```

If using Ubuntu:

```
RUN useradd -m myuser
USER myuser
```

To confirm that your container is running as a non-root user, attach to a running container and then run the `whoami` command:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ docker exec <container-id> bash
$ whoami
myuser
```

When deployed to Heroku, we also run your container as a non-root user (although we do not use the `USER` specified in the Dockerfile).

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ heroku run bash
$ whoami
U7729
```

### [Get the port from the environment variable](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#get-the-port-from-the-environment-variable)

For testing purposes, we suggest that your `Dockerfile` or code read from the $PORT environment variable, for example:

```
CMD gunicorn --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT wsgi
```

When running a Docker container locally, you can set an environment variable using the -e flag:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ docker run -p 5000:5000 -e PORT=5000 <image-name>
```

### [Setting multiple environment variables](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#setting-multiple-environment-variables)

When you use heroku locally, you can set config vars in a [.env file](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/heroku-local#set-up-your-local-environment-variables). When `heroku local` is run .env is read and each name/value pair is set in the environment. You can use this same .env file when using Docker:

Copy code to clipboard

```term
$ docker run -p 5000:5000 --env-file .env <image-name>
```

We suggest adding the .env file to your [.dockerignore file](https://docs.docker.com/build/building/context/#dockerignore-files).

### [Take advantage of Docker Compose for multi-container applications](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#take-advantage-of-docker-compose-for-multi-container-applications)

If you’ve created a multi-container application you can use Docker Compose to define your local development environment. Learn how to [use Docker Compose for local development](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/local-development-with-docker-compose).

### [Learn more](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#learn-more)

- More information on running a Docker image locally is available in [Docker’s official documentation](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/run/).
- Learn more about using [Docker Compose for local development](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/local-development-with-docker-compose).
- See the [best practices for writing Dockerfiles](https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/) guide for tips on how to optimize the build performance and size of your Docker images.

## [Heroku base images](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#heroku-base-images)

The Heroku [stacks](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/stack) are available as Docker base images for convenience (see each stack’s detail page for Docker image names and tags). However for the fastest build and boot times we strongly recommend that you instead use a smaller less-general-purpose base image, such as one of the [official Docker images](https://docs.docker.com/trusted-content/official-images/) for your app’s language.

## [Changing deployment method](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#changing-deployment-method)

Once you deploy your application via Container Registry, the stack is set to `container`. This means that your application is no longer using a [Heroku-curated stack](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/stack), but instead your own custom container.

If you no longer wish to use Docker images for your app, and would like to switch back to buildpack-based deploys using the Heroku curated stack, use the `heroku stack:set` command to switch to one of the available `heroku-*` stacks. See [migrating to a different stack](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/stack#migrating-to-a-different-stack) for more details.

## [Known issues and limitations](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime\#known-issues-and-limitations)

- We delete images that are older than 30 days if they are associated with a release that’s older than the 20 most recent releases. We also delete any images unassociated with a release within one day of artifact creation.
- Apps using the container stack must be rebuilt/redeployed in order to pick up any security updates and bug fixes to the underlying base Docker image. In contrast Heroku buildpack/slug based deployments receive automatic updates to the underlying stack’s base image, without the need to rebuild/redeploy the app.
- Heroku only supports `x86_64` images. An `unsupported architecture` error returns if you try to upload images built to run on other architectures. You also get an `Exec format` error if you try to use other architectures, like ARM64, with the Container Runtime. You must ensure your container builder outputs `x86_64` images. To ensure the built image is built for Heroku correctly, pass the platform flag to the Docker build command: `docker build --platform linux/amd64`.
- Review apps are not supported when building Docker images locally and then pushing them to the Heroku Container Registry. To use Docker with Review Apps, you must instead define your app with a [heroku.yml manifest](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/build-docker-images-heroku-yml), which allows you to build Docker images on Heroku.
- Pipeline promotions are not supported.
- While Docker images are not subject to size restrictions (unlike [slugs](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/limits#slug-size)), they are subject to the [dyno boot time restriction](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/limits#boot-timeout). As layer count/image size grows, so will dyno boot time. As such, images with more than 40 layers may fail to start in the Common Runtime. See the [best practices for writing Dockerfiles](https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/) guide for suggestions on how to reduce Docker image size.
- The `Dockerfile` commands [listed here](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/container-registry-and-runtime#unsupported-dockerfile-commands) are not supported.
- Container apps in Private or Shield spaces do not run `.profile` or `.profile.d/*` scripts when booting a dyno.
- Whiteout files (created when files from an earlier Docker image layer are deleted in a later layer) are not honoured due to the way Docker images are extracted at runtime. On the Common Runtime only, it’s possible to switch to a newer extraction method that resolves this, via `heroku labs:enable runtime-new-layer-extract`. There is currently no workaround for Private Spaces.
- The `--exit-code` argument to `heroku run` is not supported, unless [a workaround is applied](https://help.heroku.com/QUGCYVK6/how-do-i-run-one-off-dynos-with-exit-code-on-a-container-app-in-a-private-space).
- If an existing app is migrated to the container stack and the app’s process names are changed at the same time as the migration, the dynos for the old processes may persist until they are [cleaned up manually](https://help.heroku.com/W0DM3APX/unable-to-scale-or-stop-dynos-when-using-docker-containers). To avoid this, either change the process names before/after the container stack migration (rather than as part of the migration), or else scale down the old processes during the migration.
- If apps using the container stack are built using Heroku’s build system (rather than locally), then the [known issues and limitations of heroku.yml container builds](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/build-docker-images-heroku-yml#known-issues-and-limitations) also apply.
- The Heroku Container Registry only supports Docker Image Manifest V2, Schema 2 (`application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json`). It doesn’t support other manifest types, such as OCI Image Manifest (`application/vnd.oci.image.manifest.v1+json`).

### Feedback

[Log in to submit feedback.](https://devcenter.heroku.com/login?back_to=%2Farticles%2Fcontainer-registry-and-runtime&utm_campaign=login&utm_medium=feedback&utm_source=web)

metadata.description: Heroku Container Registry allows you to deploy your Docker-based app to Heroku. Both Common Runtime and Private Spaces are supported.
